---
title: "2.12 Informal Run-Time Analysis"
subtitle: "Selection and Iteration"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit2-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Topic Intro

### Count work by counting loop iterations

<span class="term">Informal run-time analysis</span> (非正式运行时间分析) asks how much work an algorithm does as the input size grows.

At this level, we count:

::: {.tight-list}
- how many times a loop body runs
- whether nested loops multiply work
- whether a triangular pattern grows like `1 + 2 + 3`
:::

## Tracing Loops

### A loop's output depends on its update

```java
int count = 0;
while (count < 5)
{
    System.out.println(count);
    count++;
}
```

The body runs five times, with `count` values `0`, `1`, `2`, `3`, `4`.

## Trace Table

### Record count, condition, and output

![While loop trace](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-12-informal-run-time-analysis/whileLoopTrace.png){fig-align="center" width="42%"}

Trace tables make iteration counts visible. They also expose off-by-one errors caused by `<` versus `<=`.

## Quick Check

### Count the iterations

How many times does the body run?

```java
int x = 1;
while (x <= 16)
{
    x *= 2;
}
```

Answer: <span class="mark">5</span> times. The values before update are `1`, `2`, `4`, `8`, `16`.

## Counting Loop Iterations

### One loop is usually proportional to its range

```java
for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++)
{
    System.out.println("*");
}
```

If `n` doubles, the number of loop iterations doubles. Informally, the work grows with `n`.

## Nested Loops

### Rectangular nested loops multiply

```java
for (int row = 1; row <= n; row++)
{
    for (int col = 1; col <= n; col++)
    {
        System.out.print("*");
    }
    System.out.println();
}
```

The inner loop runs `n` times for each of `n` rows, so the body runs `n * n` times.

## Non-Rectangular Nested Loops

### The inner count can change each row

```java
for (int row = 1; row <= n; row++)
{
    for (int col = 1; col <= row; col++)
    {
        System.out.print("*");
    }
    System.out.println();
}
```

For `n = 4`, the row counts are `1`, `2`, `3`, `4`.

## Triangular Total

### Add row counts

![Gauss summation idea](../../assets/2-selection-and-iteration/2-12-informal-run-time-analysis/sumFormula.png){fig-align="center" width="40%"}

For a triangular nested loop:

```text
1 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 10
```

In general:

```text
sum of the integers from 1 through n = n(n + 1) / 2
```

## Groupwork Analysis

### POGIL loop counting

For each code segment, identify:

::: {.tight-list}
- the loop variable
- the start value
- the stopping condition
- the update
- the number of body executions
:::

Then compare a single loop, a rectangular nested loop, and a triangular nested loop.

## Quick Check

### Compare growth

If `n = 10`, how many times does the starred print run?

```java
for (int row = 1; row <= n; row++)
{
    for (int col = 1; col <= row; col++)
    {
        System.out.print("*");
    }
}
```

Answer: `55`, because the sum from `1` through `10` is `10 * 11 / 2`.

## Classroom Check

### A complete answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- count loop iterations from initialization, condition, and update
- explain how trace tables help find iteration counts
- multiply counts for rectangular nested loops
- add row counts for triangular nested loops
- connect triangular loop counts to `n(n + 1) / 2`
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
